summary: I gave a tutorial on the Carter Family Fold and its history, which is something I often share. The Carter Family has a deep musical legacy that influenced many, including myself. Their music is part of the rich Appalachian tradition that I grew up around, especially at the Hindman Settlement School where my father worked.

My father moved to the Hindman Settlement School in 1954 to be the recreation director, and I was just six months old then. Growing up there was unique because it was not just a school but a community where students lived and worked, helping with farming, cooking, and cleaning. The school had many old buildings, some condemned, and my father was instrumental in building new ones like the May Stone building. Life there was shaped by the rural setting of Knott County, and the school played a vital role in education and community life.

Johnson City, Tennessee was important to me because it was home to WJHL-TV, the first television station we could get when I was a child. My father put up an antenna on the ridge near our home, and we would watch Channel 11 with all its local programming. I still have a relationship with people at WJHL-TV today, which shows how lasting those early connections were.

WJHL-TV in Johnson City was the first television station I could watch as a child, though the reception was often snowy and static-filled. My father rigged an antenna on the ridge to get the signal down to our house near the creek. Watching WJHL-TV was a big part of my early experience with television, and I still keep in touch with people from that station.

WSAZ-TV in Huntington, West Virginia was the best station we could get after WJHL-TV, though its signal faded in and out. Saturday nights were special because of the Saturday Night Jamboree, a program full of wonderful musicians, dancers, and singers like Connie Smith when she was just 17. Watching that show while eating tomato soup and soda crackers was a cherished childhood ritual that influenced my love of music and entertainment.

Huntington, West Virginia was home to WSAZ-TV, which was the best television station we could receive from our home near Hindman. The Saturday Night Jamboree on WSAZ was a highlight of my childhood, showcasing great musicians and performers that inspired me. Though it was some distance away, the station's programming had a strong influence on my early entertainment experiences.

I remember Connie Smith singing on WSAZ-TV’s Saturday Night Jamboree when she was just 17 years old. That show was a big part of my childhood entertainment, and her performances stood out among the wonderful musicians and dancers. Seeing young talent like Connie on TV helped shape my own aspirations to be an entertainer.

We lived near Troublesome Creek when I was a child at the Hindman Settlement School, specifically in a house called Creek House that flooded about twice a year. I remember the floods getting worse over time, with the highest flood ever recorded happening just last year. The creek and its flooding were a constant part of life growing up there, shaping how we lived and moved around.

Knott County, Kentucky is where the Hindman Settlement School is located, and it's where I spent much of my childhood. The roads were rough and travel was complicated, like the trip from Hindman to Decoy which was less than 20 miles but took about 50 miles of driving through several counties. The rural nature of Knott County shaped the school community and the lives of the students who stayed there during the week.

Decoy, Kentucky was a nearby community to Hindman in Knott County, but traveling between the two was complicated despite the short distance. Students from Decoy couldn't attend the public high school in Hindman without staying at the Settlement School during the week. This made Decoy an important part of the student population at Hindman and shaped the daily life of the school.

Breathitt County, Kentucky was close to Decoy and part of the complicated travel route students had to take to get to Hindman. Although it wasn't my home county, it factored into the regional geography that influenced how students attended school and lived at the Settlement School. The overlapping county lines made travel and schooling logistics challenging for families in the area.

Perry County, Kentucky was another neighboring county near Decoy and Hindman that students had to pass through when traveling to school. Its proximity added to the complexity of daily travel for students attending the Hindman Settlement School. These county borders shaped the educational landscape for many families in the region.

Floyd County, Kentucky was part of the route students had to take to get from Decoy to Hindman, even though both are in Knott County. This detour through Floyd and other counties made what should have been a short trip into a long journey. It highlights how isolated and rural the area was when I was growing up.

My father was instrumental in building the May Stone building at the Hindman Settlement School. When he became director, many of the campus buildings were condemned, and he worked hard to improve the facilities. The May Stone building was part of his legacy in making the school a better place for students and staff.

John F. Kennedy was mentioned in the context of the era I grew up in, but there were no direct personal memories or experiences related to him in my story. The focus was more on local community life and the musical and educational environment around the Hindman Settlement School during the 1950s and 60s.
